prompt: Explain photosynthesis to a curious adult non-scientist in around 150 words. Avoid a bulleted list.
response: Imagine every green leaf as a quiet chef. From sunrise to sunset it takes invisible carbon dioxide from the air, sips water drawn up through its roots, and faces the sky to catch falling sunlight. Inside the leaf, tiny solar panels called chlorophyll bend that light, using its energy to break water apart and glue the leftover hydrogen onto carbon dioxide. The elegant leftovers are sugar—sweet fuel the plant uses to build every root, shoot, flower, and fruit—and oxygen, released back to us as an effortless thank-you gift. So every breath you take and every bite of food you enjoy circles back to this microscopic kitchen happening in green things everywhere, turning plain air and water into life’s larder while quietly keeping the sky stocked with the oxygen we need.
